summary: Ronacher argues that Python modules are de facto singletons, and that the real problem with singletons is shared global state. He contrasts Python modules with C# namespaces to show how Python’s module objects can store mutable state, which then becomes globally shared through `sys.modules`. This global cache makes module reloading, multi-version dependencies, and multiple app instances in one interpreter effectively impossible without unsafe hacks. He proposes an idea—tagged modules in `sys.modules`—to allow multiple versions or instances, but notes it cannot be made backward compatible. He concludes that the post is more a problem statement and brainstorming than a concrete fix, and hints the issue is broader than Python alone.
key_points: Python modules are singletons because imports are cached in `sys.modules`, creating global shared state.
Shared state in module scope causes real-world problems in stdlib and frameworks (e.g., reinitialization, configuration clashes).
Hot-reloading or upgrading code in long-running processes is dangerous because modules cannot be cleanly unloaded.
Single-module globality blocks multi-version dependencies and multiple app instances within one interpreter.
A hypothetical tagging system for module imports could mitigate this, but it is incompatible with existing Python behavior.
money_quotes: A singleton is nothing more than a glorified global variable.
That's right folks: a Python module is a singleton.
There can only be one version of a module, what does this mean for us?
The funny (and sad) part is that all these nice things do not work just because of one single object: `sys.modules`, the übersingleton of Python.
So if we can't get rid of the singleton, add some more!
key_insight: Python’s module system is a global singleton cache, and that shared state makes reloading, multi-version dependencies, and multi-instance apps fundamentally hard to do safely.
